Chapter 2 — Biomechanics of Resistance Exercise

Biomechanics
of Resistance
Exercise

Part A — Skeletal Musculature & Levers

Topic 01

Skeletal
Musculature

How muscles attach to bone, how those attachments are defined, and the roles each muscle plays during movement.

Topic 01 — Skeletal Musculature

Origin & Insertion — Proximal & Distal Attachments

Origin — Proximal Attachment
More Stationary End
Toward the centre of the body. Traditionally the end that moves less during the movement.
Insertion — Distal Attachment
More Mobile End
Away from the centre of the body. The end that moves more during the movement.

Why it can reverse: In a straight-leg sit-up, the femur is relatively immobile — so the femur becomes the origin of the iliacus, and the more mobile pelvis becomes the insertion. But in a leg raise, the pelvis is fixed against the bench — so the pelvis becomes the origin and the femur (moving) becomes the insertion. The traditional proximal/distal definition provides the most consistency.

Two attachment types: Fleshy attachments — muscle fibres affix directly to bone over a wide area, distributing force. Fibrous attachments (tendons) — blend into both muscle sheath and bone connective tissue with additional fibres extending into bone, making a very strong union.

fitbudd.com
Topic 01 — Skeletal Musculature
Muscle Roles in Movement
Prime Mover
Agonist
Most directly responsible for producing the movement.

Biceps brachii during elbow flexion.
Opposing Muscle
Antagonist
Slows/stops the movement. Brakes the limb at end range, protecting joint structures.

Triceps brachii during elbow flexion.
Indirect Assist
Synergist
Assists indirectly — stabilises a proximal segment so the agonist can act effectively.

Brachialis during elbow flexion.

Virtually all body movements involve more than one muscle. The gluteus maximus must act synergistically during a squat rise to counteract hip flexion that would otherwise result from tension in the rectus femoris.

Topic 02

Levers &
Mechanical
Advantage

Understanding how the musculoskeletal system uses lever mechanics to produce torque — and why most muscles operate at a mechanical disadvantage.

Topic 02 — Levers & Mechanical Advantage

What is a Lever?

Lever
Rigid or Semi-Rigid Body
When subjected to a force whose line of action does not pass through its pivot point, a lever exerts force on any object impeding its tendency to rotate. In the body: bones are the levers.
Fulcrum
The Pivot Point
The fixed point about which the lever rotates. In the body: joints are the fulcrums. The elbow joint is the fulcrum for forearm movements.
Moment Arm
Perpendicular Distance
The perpendicular distance from the line of action of the force to the fulcrum. Also called force arm, lever arm, or torque arm. Longer moment arm = more torque for the same force.

Although many muscles in the body do not act through levers (e.g. face, tongue, heart, sphincters), body movements directly involved in sport and exercise primarily act through the bony levers of the skeleton.

Topic 02 — Levers

Levers are Everywhere

Before we look at the body, recognise lever classes in familiar objects.

CLASS 1
Scissors — fulcrum between
effort & resistance
CLASS 2
Wheelbarrow — load between
fulcrum & effort
CLASS 3
Stapler — effort between
fulcrum & resistance
Topic 02 — Lever Classes

The FLE Rule — Identifying Lever Classes

In each lever, three elements are present: Fulcrum, Load (resistance), Effort (muscle force). Their arrangement determines the class.

1
F — L & E opposite
Fulcrum is between load and effort.
Order: E — F — L or L — F — E

Example: Scissors, seesaw
Body: Triceps extension (elbow), neck extension
2
L between F & E
Load is between fulcrum and effort.
Order: F — L — E

Example: Wheelbarrow
Body: Standing heel raise (calf raise)
3
E between F & L
Effort is between fulcrum and load.
Order: F — E — L

Example: Stapler, tweezers
Body: Biceps curl — most common in human body

⚠️ The classification of a lever often depends on the somewhat arbitrary decision of where the fulcrum lies. Understanding the principle of mechanical advantage is of much greater importance than being able to classify levers.

Topic 02 — Torque

Torque — The Rotational Force

The Torque Equation
τ = F × d
τ
Torque / Moment
(N·m)
F
Applied Force
(N)
d
Moment Arm
(m)
What Torque Means
Torque is the degree to which a force tends to rotate an object about a fulcrum. Applying 100 N at 0.5 m produces the same torque (50 N·m) as applying 50 N at 1.0 m. Greater moment arm = more torque for the same force.
Equilibrium Condition
For a joint to be in equilibrium (isometric or constant-speed): FM × MM = FR × MR

Muscle torque must exactly balance resistive torque. This equation governs every resistance exercise.
Topic 02 — Mechanical Advantage

Mechanical Advantage

The Formula
MA = MM ÷ MR
MA
Mechanical
Advantage
MM
Muscle
Moment Arm
MR
Resistive
Moment Arm
MA > 1.0
Mechanical Advantage
Muscle moment arm is longer than resistive moment arm. Muscle force needed is less than the external load. Class 2 levers only.
MA < 1.0
Mechanical Disadvantage
Muscle moment arm is shorter than resistive moment arm. Muscle must generate force greater than the external load. Most human muscles — Class 3.
Key Implication
Internal Forces Are Huge
When MR is 8× MM, muscle must exert 8× the resistive force. This explains why tendons and muscles experience forces far exceeding the weight being lifted — a primary driver of soft tissue injury.
trainingstation.co.uk
Topic 02 — First-Class Lever
Class 1 Lever — Forces on Opposite Sides

Muscle force (FM) and resistive force (FR) act on opposite sides of the fulcrum. Like a seesaw.

Body Example
Triceps pushdown. Elbow = fulcrum. Triceps pulls on one side; resistance (cable) acts on the other side.
Mechanical Advantage
MA = MM/MR = 5 cm / 40 cm = 0.125
Much less than 1.0 — a large muscle force is needed to produce a small resistive torque.

In Figure 2.1, because MM is much smaller than MR, FM must be much greater than FR — this illustrates the disadvantageous nature of this arrangement.

sat-congo.com
Topic 02 — Second-Class Lever
Class 2 Lever — Muscle Arm Longer

Both forces on the same side of the fulcrum, with the muscle moment arm longer than the resistive moment arm.

Body Example
Standing heel raise. Ball of foot = fulcrum. Calf raises the body (resistance = body weight). MM > MR, so FM < FR.
Mechanical Advantage
MA > 1.0 — the calf muscle exerts less force than body weight. This class is rare in the human body.

Second-class levers are the only class where the muscle has a genuine mechanical advantage over the resistive load.

healthshots.com
Topic 02 — Third-Class Lever
Class 3 Lever — Muscle Arm Shorter

Both forces on the same side of the fulcrum, with the muscle moment arm shorter than the resistive moment arm.

Body Example
Biceps curl. Elbow = fulcrum. Biceps inserts close to the joint; dumbbell is held far away. MM ≪ MR, so FM ≫ FR.
Mechanical Advantage
MA < 1.0 — muscle must generate force much greater than the dumbbell weight. Most common class in the human body.

The mechanical disadvantage explains why muscles and tendons experience forces far exceeding the external load — a major contributor to soft tissue injury when volume and intensity are mismanaged.

healthdirect.gov.au
Topic 02 — Mechanical Advantage
The Patella & Variations in Tendon Insertion

The patella maintains the quadriceps tendon's distance from the knee's axis of rotation — preventing the moment arm from collapsing as the joint flexes.

Farther Insertion
More Torque, Less Speed
Longer moment arm → greater torque per unit of muscle force. Advantageous for slow, heavy movements (powerlifting).
Closer Insertion
Less Torque, More Speed
Less muscle shortening per degree of joint rotation → faster movement. Advantageous for high-speed athletic activities.

Non-modifiable structure — tendon insertion points are determined by genetics. This force–velocity trade-off explains why athletes of similar muscle mass can differ dramatically in strength vs. speed.

Topic 02 — Levers & Mechanical Advantage

Three Lever Classes — Comparison

Class 1 — Scissors
Forces Opposite Sides
Order: E – F – L (or L – F – E)
MA: Usually < 1.0
Body: Triceps pushdown, neck extension
Effect: Large muscle force needed
Class 2 — Wheelbarrow
Load Between F & E
Order: F – L – E
MA: > 1.0
Body: Standing heel raise
Effect: Muscle force < load — rare
Class 3 — Stapler
Effort Between F & L
Order: F – E – L
MA: < 1.0
Body: Biceps curl, most limb muscles
Effect: Muscle force ≫ load — most common

Most human muscles operate at a mechanical disadvantage. Internal forces in muscles and tendons far exceed the external load. This is both why resistance training creates adaptation — and why technique, volume, and progression management are critical for injury prevention.

Topic 02 — Textbook Reference

Figures 2.1, 2.3, 2.4 — Textbook Diagrams

Fig 2.1 — Class 1
Triceps extension. MA = 5/40 = 0.125
Fig 2.3 — Class 2
Heel raise. MM > MR, so FM < FR
Fig 2.4 — Class 3
Biceps curl. MM ≪ MR, so FM ≫ FR
Topic 03

Anatomical
Planes &
Body Movements

The three reference planes used to describe human movement, the major joint actions within each plane, and how exercise selection maps to sport-specific demands.

physiquedevelopment.com
Topic 03 — Anatomical Planes
The Three Reference Planes

In the standard anatomical position (standing upright, arms at sides, palms forward), all movement is described relative to three planes.

Sagittal Plane
Divides left–right. Movements: flexion / extension
Examples: squat, deadlift, biceps curl, running stride
Frontal Plane
Divides front–back. Movements: abduction / adduction, lateral flexion
Examples: lateral raise, side lunge, lateral bending
Transverse Plane
Divides upper–lower. Movements: rotation, pronation / supination
Examples: dumbbell fly, Russian twist, golf swing

Few movements occur in a single plane in isolation. Compound exercises cross multiple planes simultaneously. Training all three planes is essential for complete athletic development.

Fig 2.10 — NSCA CSCS 5th Ed.
Topic 03 — Body Movements
Major Body Movements (1 of 2)

Fig 2.10 maps joint actions across all three planes for the wrist, elbow, forearm, shoulder, and neck — each linked to exercises and sport examples.

Sagittal
Wrist: flex/extend · Elbow: biceps curl / triceps pushdown · Shoulder: front raise / pullover
Frontal
Wrist: ulnar/radial deviation · Shoulder: lat pulldown / lateral raise · Scapula: shrug / depression
Transverse
Forearm: pronation / supination · Shoulder: internal/external rotation · Scapula: retraction / protraction
Fig 2.10 cont. — NSCA CSCS 5th Ed.
Topic 03 — Body Movements
Major Body Movements (2 of 2)

Lower body movements — lower back, hip, knee, and ankle — with exercise and sport examples.

Hip — Sagittal
Flexion: leg raise, squat (eccentric) · Extension: squat (concentric), glute bridge, sprinting, weightlifting pull
Knee & Ankle
Knee flex/extend: leg curl / squat · Dorsiflexion / plantar flexion: band resistance / calf raise, jump take-off
Often Omitted
Shoulder rotation, knee flexion, hip internal/external rotation, ankle dorsiflexion, hip adduction/abduction, torso transverse rotation — all critical in sport.

Dynamic correspondence — match exercise plane, amplitude, velocity, and timing to the target sport movement for maximal transfer.

Topic 04

Human
Strength
& Power

Newton's Laws, force, work, energy, power, impulse, momentum — the mechanical foundations of human movement.

Topic 04 — Human Strength & Power

Newton's Three Laws of Motion

1
Law of Inertia
An object at rest stays at rest. An object in motion stays in motion. Nothing changes unless a force acts on it.
A barbell on the floor won't move until you apply force. A sprinter decelerates only because friction and air resistance act on them.
2
Law of Acceleration
Force = mass × acceleration. The greater the force you apply, the greater the acceleration — for the same mass.
A heavier barbell requires more force to accelerate at the same rate. A lighter implement accelerates more for the same muscular effort.
3
Law of Reaction
For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction. Forces always come in pairs.
You push down on the ground — the ground pushes back up with equal force (ground reaction force). That upward force is what propels you off the ground during a jump.
Topic 04 — Human Strength & Power

From F = ma to Impulse–Momentum

1
F = m × a
Newton's 2nd Law. Force equals mass times acceleration. (Eq 2.1)
↓
2
Acceleration = Δv ÷ Δt
Acceleration is the change in velocity (Δv) divided by the time taken (Δt).
↓
3
F = m × (Δv ÷ Δt)
Substitute the definition of acceleration into Eq 2.1.
↓
✓
F × Δt = m × Δv
Multiply both sides by Δt. Left = Impulse. Right = change in Momentum. (Eq 2.2)

Impulse (F × Δt) and momentum (m × Δv) are both vector quantities — they have magnitude and direction. To jump higher, you need more impulse. You can get it from more force, more time, or both.

Topic 04 — Human Strength & Power

Work, Power & Energy — What Are They?

🏋️
Work
What happens when a force moves something. If you push hard but nothing moves — no work is done. Work = Force × Distance moved.
⚡
Energy
The capacity to do work. Exists as potential energy (stored in position) or kinetic energy (stored in motion). Work changes an object's energy.
🚀
Power
How quickly work is done. Two athletes can do the same work — but the one who does it faster produces more power. Power = Work ÷ Time.

Work and power are scalar quantities — they have magnitude only, no direction. For a complete picture of sport performance, they must be paired with vector quantities like impulse and momentum.

Topic 04 — Work

Work — Force × Displacement

Equation 2.3 — Work
W = F × d
W
Work
Joules (J)
F
Force
Newtons (N)
d
Displacement
Metres (m)
Worked Example
A 60 kg barbell pressed overhead 0.541 m:
W = 60 × 9.81 × 0.541 = 318.4 J

Lowering phase: displacement is negative → W = −318.4 J (negative work).
Work = Change in Energy
Work also equals the change in mechanical energy of an object:
Potential energy = m × g × h
Kinetic energy = ½ × m × v²
Work = ΔPE + ΔKE
Topic 04 — Energy

Potential & Kinetic Energy

Potential Energy (Eq 2.3)
PE = m × g × h
m
Mass (kg)
g
9.81 m·s⁻²
h
Height (m)
Kinetic Energy (Eq 2.4)
KE = ½ × m × v²
m
Mass (kg)
v²
Velocity²
(m·s⁻¹)²
Potential Energy
Energy stored due to position. A barbell at the top of an overhead press has more PE than at the bottom. When lowered, PE converts to KE.
Kinetic Energy
Energy stored due to motion. A sprinter at top speed has high KE. A stationary barbell has zero KE. Both forms sum to total mechanical energy.
Topic 04 — Power

Power — Work Per Unit Time

Power
P = W ÷ t  =  F × v
P
Power
Watts (W)
W
Work (J)
t
Time (s)
F
Force (N)
v
Velocity (m·s⁻¹)
Worked Example
60 kg bar, 0.541 m, 0.815 s:
P = 318.4 ÷ 0.815 = 390.7 W
Mean power for the concentric phase.
Powerlifting vs Weightlifting
Despite the name, powerlifting produces less mechanical power than weightlifting — slow movement = low velocity = low P = F × v.
Peak vs Mean Power
Peak power = work done in 1 ms window. Mean power = over the full phase. Mean power is more practically relevant for training load.
Topic 04 — Human Strength & Power

Strength vs Power — The Distinction

Strength
Capacity to Exert Force
The ability to produce force at any velocity — not just slow movements. Low-velocity strength is critical when opposing player inertia slows movement (e.g. linemen).
Power
Force × Velocity
Rate of work. High-velocity strength critical when resistance is low and rapid adjustments are needed (e.g. badminton stroke).
Key Insight
Neither Is Superior
Strength and power reflect ability to exert force at different velocities. Sport demands determine which matters most. Both are trainable and both are necessary.
Exam Trap
Powerlifting ≠ Most Power
Weightlifting (snatch, clean & jerk) produces higher mechanical power output than powerlifting because key phases are time-constrained.
srgneetacademy / Facebook
Topic 04 — Angular Motion
Torque, Angular Displacement & Angular Impulse
τ = F × d × sin θ   |   τ × Δt ∝ Δω
Angular Displacement θ
Measured in radians. 1 radian = 57.3°. Rate of change = angular velocity (ω) in rad·s⁻¹.
Torque Peaks at 90°
sin 90° = 1.0 → maximum torque when forearm is horizontal. Drops toward zero at full extension or full flexion.
Angular Impulse
τ × Δt drives change in angular velocity. More torque or more time = greater angular impulse = faster rotation.
Part B Summary — Topics 03 & 04

Key Takeaways

Anatomical Planes
  • Sagittal = flexion/extension (most common in standard programs)
  • Frontal = abduction/adduction (often undertrained)
  • Transverse = rotation (most sport-critical, most neglected)
  • Dynamic correspondence = match plane + timing to sport movement
Key Equations
  • F = ma — Newton's 2nd Law
  • F × Δt = m × Δv — Impulse = change in momentum (vector)
  • W = F × d — Work in joules
  • P = W/t = F × v — Power in watts
  • τ = F × d × sin θ — Torque varies with joint angle
Newton's Laws
1 — Inertia: nothing changes without force · 2 — F = ma: more force = more acceleration · 3 — Reaction: every force has an equal and opposite reaction (GRF during jump)
Exam Traps
  • Powerlifting ≠ highest power (weightlifting is higher)
  • Work and power are scalars — no direction
  • Peak power ≠ mean power
  • Impulse & momentum are vectors — direction matters
Topic 05

Biomechanical
Factors in
Human Strength

The structural and mechanical variables that determine how much force a muscle can produce — and how that changes across training, body size, and movement context.

Topic 05 — Biomechanical Factors in Strength

What Determines Muscle Strength?

1
Neural Control
Recruitment (which motor units) + rate coding (how fast they fire)
2
Muscle Cross-Sectional Area
Force ∝ CSA, not volume. Bigger ≠ proportionally stronger per kg
3
Fibre Arrangement & Pennation
Angle of pennation — trade-off between force capacity and shortening velocity
4
Muscle Length
Resting length = maximum crossbridge sites = maximum force
5
Joint Angle & Exercise Technique
Torque varies throughout ROM. Technique shifts load between joints
6
Muscle Contraction Velocity
Force declines as concentric velocity increases (Hill's law)
7
Joint Angular Velocity
Three muscle action types — concentric, eccentric, isometric
8
Strength-to-Mass Ratio
Body size scaling — CSA ∝ dim², mass ∝ dim³. Smaller athletes stronger per kg
Topic 05 — Factors 1 & 2

Neural Control & Muscle Cross-Sectional Area

Recruitment
Which & How Many Motor Units
More motor units recruited = greater force. Early training gains (weeks 1–4) are primarily neural — the brain learns to recruit more contractile tissue efficiently before significant hypertrophy occurs.
Rate Coding
How Fast They Fire
Higher firing rate = greater force per motor unit. Novices who plateau early should continue — hypertrophy mechanisms take over as neural adaptations mature.
CSA — The Key Principle
Force ∝ Area, Not Volume
Two athletes with the same biceps circumference but different heights have approximately the same biceps strength — same CSA, different volume. Taller = heavier = weaker strength-to-mass ratio.

⚠️ Exam trap: Elite gymnasts are short because their sport demands a high strength-to-bodyweight ratio. Larger body size means mass grows faster than CSA — lowering relative strength.

Resistance training increases both CSA and neural efficiency. Strength gains initially outpace hypertrophy because neural adaptations are faster.

sems-journal.ch
Topic 05 — Factor 2
Muscle Cross-Sectional Area — Structural Detail

Maximally contracting muscles generate 16–100 N/cm² of CSA. The wide range is explained by fibre arrangement, pennation angle, and fibre type.

Why Area Not Volume
Sarcomeres in parallel sum their forces — each adds to the total force across the cross-section. Sarcomeres in series sum their velocities, not forces. More CSA = more parallel sarcomeres = more force.
Resistance Training Effect
Training increases both strength and CSA. The two are tightly linked — but not proportionally in the short term. Early gains are neural; later gains are structural.
Body Size Implication
Taller athletes have more muscle volume but not necessarily more CSA in proportion. As body size increases, mass grows faster than strength — smaller athletes are stronger pound-for-pound.
Pennation Influence
Pennate muscles pack more fibres per unit volume — greater physiological CSA. Angle of pennation is modifiable through training, partially explaining why strength and speed differences emerge in athletes of similar apparent size.
Fig 2.11 — NSCA CSCS 5th Ed.
Topic 05 — Factor 3
Fibre Arrangement & Angle of Pennation
Pennate Muscle
Fibres align obliquely to the tendon. Angle of pennation = angle between fibres and origin-to-insertion line. 0° = no pennation. Most muscles <15°. Increases as muscle shortens.
More Pennation
More sarcomeres in parallel → greater force. Fewer in series → lower max velocity. Better for eccentric, isometric, low-speed concentric.
Less Pennation
More sarcomeres in series → higher shortening velocity. Better for high-velocity movements. Less raw force capacity.
Titin
3rd contractile element. Tightens during eccentric stretch → increases Ca²⁺ sensitivity → aligns myosin → boosts crossbridge formation. Key in eccentric force production beyond resting length.
Fig 2.12 — NSCA CSCS 5th Ed.
Topic 05 — Factor 4
Muscle Length — Length–Tension Relationship
At Resting Length
Maximum actin–myosin overlap. Most crossbridge sites available. Peak force generation.
Stretched Beyond Resting
Fewer crossbridge sites. Force drops. Titin activates → Ca²⁺ ↑ → aligns myosin → partially compensates. Key in eccentric actions.
Shortened Below Resting
Actin filaments overlap excessively → fewer functional crossbridge sites → force decreases.

Exam note: Titin is the third contractile element alongside actin and myosin. It contributes to structural alignment, trophic signalling, and eccentric force production beyond resting length.

openoregon.pressbooks.pub
Topic 05 — Factor 5a
Joint Angle — Torque Varies Through ROM

The torque a muscle can produce changes throughout the range of motion — driven by changing moment arm length and changing muscle length simultaneously.

Resistive Torque
For free weights: resistive moment arm = horizontal distance from weight to joint. Maximum resistive torque when forearm is horizontal (90°). Zero when weight is directly above or below joint.
Sticking Point
During isokinetic exercise, torque peaks at ~90° then declines. Sticking point = where muscle torque capacity is lowest relative to resistive torque. During biceps curl, "cheating" allows eccentrics to continue past this point.
During Dynamic Movement
Mechanical advantage changes continuously. For movements like knee extension, the patella prevents large changes in moment arm — maintaining more consistent torque across the ROM.
Training Implication
Understanding torque-angle curves helps S&C coaches select exercises and equipment that provide appropriate loading at the right point in the ROM for each athlete's goal.
reddit.com/r/weightlifting
Topic 05 — Factor 5b
Exercise Technique — Shifts Torque Between Joints

Technique changes the horizontal distance from the weight to each joint — redistributing the resistive torque that different muscle groups must produce.

High Bar Squat
More upright trunk → weight closer to knees → greater knee extensor demand (quadriceps). Knees travel further forward. More ankle dorsiflexion required.
Low Bar Squat
More forward trunk lean → weight closer to hips, farther from knees → greater hip extensor demand (glutes + hamstrings). Reduced knee torque, increased hip torque.
General Principle
Technique is not just aesthetic — it is a mechanical decision about which muscles bear the load. S&C coaches select technique based on the athlete's sport demands and injury history.
Same Applies to Deadlifts
Conventional vs sumo deadlift: hip width and trunk angle alter torque at the hip, knee, and spine. No universal "correct" technique — mechanics should match athlete structure and goals.
youtube.com
Topic 05 — Factor 6
Force–Velocity Relationship & Muscle Action Types

Classic A.V. Hill experiments: force capability declines as velocity of shortening increases. The decline is steepest at lower speeds.

Concentric
Muscle shortens. Force declines as velocity increases. Force capability approaches zero at maximum unloaded velocity.
Eccentric
Muscle lengthens. Force increases above isometric as velocity increases. Occurs during all lowering phases. Greater injury potential.
Isometric
No length change. Contractile = resistive force. The midpoint of the force-velocity curve. Benchmark for comparing concentric and eccentric values.

⚠️ "Muscle action" is preferred over "contraction" — the word contraction implies shortening, which doesn't describe eccentric or isometric actions accurately.

Topic 05 — Factors 7 & 8

Strength-to-Mass Ratio & Body Size Scaling

The Scaling Problem
CSA vs Mass
  • Force ∝ CSA → scales to 2nd power of body dimensions
  • Body mass ∝ volume → scales to 3rd power
  • As body grows, mass outpaces strength
  • Smaller athletes are stronger pound-for-pound
Sport Implication
Sprint & Jump
In sprinting and jumping: strength of muscles ÷ mass being accelerated is what matters. A sprinter who gains 15% more mass gains proportional strength — but the ratio may not improve and speed suffers.
Classic Formula
Load ÷ BW2/3
Accounts for the expected drop in relative strength with size. Corrects for CSA-vs-volume scaling — allowing fairer comparison across weight classes in lifting sports.
Weight Class Selection
Find Your Competitive Class
Athletes should find the class where their strength is highest relative to others. Medium-weight athletes often perform best by classic formula — normal distribution clusters most people near the mean.

Strength-to-mass ratio directly reflects an athlete's ability to accelerate their own body. The strongest absolute lifter is not necessarily the best jumper or sprinter.

Topic 05 — Exam Summary

Biomechanical Factors — Exam Summary

Neural Factors (1–2)
  • Recruitment: which/how many motor units
  • Rate coding: how fast they fire
  • Early training gains = neural, not hypertrophy
  • Force ∝ CSA not volume
Structural Factors (3–4)
  • Greater pennation → more force, less speed
  • Resting length = max crossbridge sites = max force
  • Titin = 3rd contractile element (eccentric)
  • 16–100 N/cm² force capability range
Mechanical Factors (5–6)
  • Torque varies non-linearly through ROM
  • Sticking point = lowest muscle torque in ROM
  • Technique shifts torque between joints
  • Eccentric > isometric > concentric force
Body Size (7–8)
  • Force ∝ dim² | Mass ∝ dim³
  • Smaller athletes stronger per kg
  • Classic formula: load ÷ BW2/3
  • Strength-to-mass ratio = acceleration capacity
Exam Traps
  • Use "muscle action" not "contraction"
  • Titin is a contractile element, not just structural
  • Eccentric force > isometric force (not less)
  • High-bar ≠ better squat — mechanics matter
Titin — Critical Detail
  • Spring-like protein alongside actin & myosin
  • Tightens eccentrically → ↑ Ca²⁺ → ↑ crossbridges
  • Trophic signalling → hypertrophy contribution
  • Active beyond resting length, not just structural
Topic 06

Sources of
Resistance to
Muscle Contraction

Gravity, inertia, friction, fluid resistance, and elasticity — how each creates a distinct force pattern through the range of motion.

Topic 06 — Sources of Resistance

Five Sources of Resistance

🏋️ Gravity
Free weights · weight-stack machines
Always acts downward
💨 Inertia
Accelerating loads
Acts in any direction
🛷 Friction
Sleds · ergometers
F = k × F_N
🌊 Fluid
Hydraulic machines · swimming
F = k × v
kettlebellkings.com
Topic 06 — Gravity
Gravity — Fg = m × ag
Fg = m × 9.81 m·s⁻²
Key Rule
Resistive moment arm = horizontal distance from the weight to the joint. Zero resistive torque when weight is directly above or below the joint.
Mass ≠ Weight
85 kg barbell = 833.85 N on Earth. Same mass, lighter on moon. Refer to barbells by mass (kg), not weight.
Low vs High Bar Squat
Low bar: trunk forward → ↓ knee torque, ↑ hip torque. High bar: more upright → greater knee extensor demand.
Cam Machines
Variable-radius cam changes moment arm through ROM. Goal: match resistance to torque curve. Often fails to match normal human patterns.
mirafit.co.uk
Topic 06 — Inertia
Inertial Force Pattern Through ROM
Finertial = m × a  |  acts in any direction
Early ROM
During acceleration: resistance exceeds bar weight. Agonist muscles receive greater-than-weight resistance at the start.
Late ROM
During deceleration: resistance drops below bar weight. Antagonist muscles or reduced force decelerate the load.
Explosive vs Slow
Higher acceleration → greater inertial resistance early in ROM. Heavier weights possible in explosive exercises — providing near-maximal stimulus across more muscles.
Training Application
Acceleration is characteristic of natural sport movements. Resistance training involving acceleration produces desirable neuromuscular adaptations for sport.
worldathletics.org
Topic 06 — Inertia
The Bracketing Technique

Train the movement with both heavier and lighter loads than normal to develop the full force–velocity spectrum.

Heavier Load
Greater inertia → forces muscle to contract at low velocity. Per the force–velocity relationship, low velocity = high force output. Develops strength end of spectrum.
Lighter Load
Less inertia → allows higher velocity of movement and higher release speed. Trains the neuromuscular system to operate at desired velocity ranges.
Shot Put Example
Heavier shot → greater force during acceleration phase. Lighter shot → higher speed of release. Both around competition weight to bracket the target movement.
Caution
Changing implement loading affects motor pattern. Body needs time to adjust. Technique-oriented sports (e.g. throwing, sprinting) require careful application.
miamispineandperformance.com
Topic 06 — Inertia
Olympic Lifts — Time-Constrained Force Application

The snatch and clean & jerk are effective for developing high-acceleration capacity against heavy resistance because key phases are time-constrained by how the lifter interacts with the barbell.

Why Time-Constrained
In weightlifting, the lifter must generate enough impulse to get under the bar before it descends. This forces application of very high force in very short time windows.
vs Powerlifting
Powerlifting movements are not time-constrained in the same way — the lifter can grind slowly through the range. Lower velocity = lower mechanical power output.
Momentum Contribution
During a power clean, strong legs and hips accelerate the bar to high velocity. Bar momentum then carries it upward even as the weaker upper body transitions — the bar's momentum does the work.
Training Rationale
For athletes requiring rapid force development (team sports, jumpers, throwers), Olympic lifts provide sport-specific neuromuscular stimulus unavailable with slow-tempo lifting.
simplifaster.com
Topic 06 — Friction
Friction — FR = k × FN
FR = k × FN
k = Friction Coefficient
Static k always greater than dynamic k. More force needed to start moving than to keep moving. Varies with surface (grass, turf, concrete) and sled runners.
FN = Normal Force
Force pressing surfaces together. Adding plates to the sled increases FN → increases friction resistance. Pushing upward reduces FN slightly, reducing friction.
Horizontal Resistance
Sleds provide horizontal resistance — impossible with free weights alone. Directly replicates the force direction of sprinting, blocking, and lateral movement in sport.
Variable Consistency
Friction coefficient varies with surface and conditions. Sled training outdoors provides non-repeatable resistance — useful for general overload but less precise than machine-based training.
simplifaster.com
Topic 06 — Fluid Resistance
Fluid Resistance — FR = k × v
FR = k × v
Velocity-Proportional
Resistance increases with speed of movement. Allows rapid initial acceleration then self-limits — movement velocity stays in an intermediate range.
No Eccentric Phase
Without an internal pump, the antagonist works concentrically on the return stroke — no eccentric loading of the agonist. Limits specificity for sport movements involving eccentric action.
Surface & Form Drag
Surface drag: friction of fluid along object surface. Form drag: fluid pressing against front/rear of object. Cross-sectional area has major effect on form drag.
Not Truly Isokinetic
Fluid machines are sometimes marketed as isokinetic. They are not — they limit velocity changes but do not maintain a truly constant speed throughout the movement.
health.com
Topic 06 — Elasticity
Elasticity — FR = k × x
FR = k × x
Resistance Pattern
Begins low, ends high — opposite to human torque curves, which show a substantial drop-off toward end of range of motion.
Jump Training Limitation
Band most stretched = greatest resistance when airborne. At this point large muscles have already completed their work — band simply pulls athlete back to ground faster, increasing landing velocity.
Adjustability Problem
Resistance adjustability is limited to the number of elastic components available. Fine-tuning load is difficult compared to weight stack or hydraulic machines.
Appropriate Uses
Bands are useful for activation work, mobility, and accommodating resistance in compound lifts — where the highest band tension coincides with the athlete's strongest joint angle.
Topic 07

Joint
Biomechanics
& Injury Risk

Back, shoulders, knees, elbows, and wrists — anatomical vulnerability, force mechanics, and evidence-based injury prevention for resistance training.

painguru.in
Topic 07 — Back
Disk Herniation — L4/L5 & L5/S1
85–90% of Herniations
Occur at L4/L5 or L5/S1. These disks bear the greatest compressive forces — they sit at the base of the spine's lever arm, where erector forces concentrate.
Why So Vulnerable
Erector spinae moment arm ≈ 5 cm. Weight moment arm is far longer. Erectors must generate up to 10× the weight lifted — concentrating enormous compressive force at the lowest disks.
Rounded Back
Ventral (anterior) disk edges are squeezed → nucleus pulposus is pushed posteriorly → herniation risk. The most dangerous lifting posture.
Extreme Arch
Dorsal (posterior) disk edges squeezed. Also increases risk. Neutral lordotic spine keeps disk geometry flat — minimising asymmetric compression.
builtwithscience.com
Topic 07 — Back
Neutral Spine & Intra-Abdominal Pressure
Neutral / Lordotic Position
Best overall posture for lifting. Keeps intervertebral disks flat. Minimises L5/S1 compressive forces and ligament strain. Low back muscles exert considerably higher forces when the back is arched vs neutral.
Fluid Ball (IAP)
Deep abdominals + diaphragm contract → pressurised fluid ball in abdominal cavity → internal support for vertebral column. Significantly reduces erector spinae forces and disk compression.

⚠️ Deep squatting with load is not necessarily associated with back injury — spinal loads vary with posture. Rounding the lower back under heavy load is the primary risk factor.

richgrinds.com
Topic 07 — Back
Valsalva Maneuver & Lifting Belts
Valsalva Maneuver
Glottis closed → air trapped → abdominal + rib cage contract → rigid torso. Increases rigidity for heavy loads.
⚠️ Compresses heart → transiently raises blood pressure.
Safer Alternative
Diaphragm + abdominals contract with glottis open. Creates fluid ball without pressurising chest. Recommended for most resistance training. IAP builds reflexively even with open airway.
Lifting Belts
Increase IAP → reduce erector forces → reduce disk compression. Effective when used correctly.
⚠️ Constant use weakens deep abdominals.
Recommendations
No belt for non-back exercises. For back exercises: no belt for lighter sets, belt for near-maximal/maximal sets only. Many world-class weightlifters never wear belts.
kenhub.com
Topic 07 — Shoulders
Shoulder Joint — Greatest ROM, Least Stability
Glenoid Cavity
Not a true socket — significantly less stable than the hip. The glenoid is shallow, making it dependent on surrounding soft tissue for stability.
Greatest ROM
Widest range of motion of any body joint. This mobility is also the source of its vulnerability — structures providing stability are also those most susceptible to impingement and tearing.
Stability Structures
Glenoid labrum, joint synovium, capsule, ligaments, rotator cuff muscles, tendons, and bursae. Any of these can be damaged by high training forces or improper technique.
Risk Management
Warm up with light weights before bench/shoulder press. Train all major shoulder movement planes for balanced development. Avoid excessive loading on impingement-prone positions.
kenhub.com
Topic 07 — Shoulders
The Rotator Cuff
Four Muscles
Supraspinatus · Infraspinatus · Teres minor · Subscapularis (SITS). Together with the pectorals, they keep the humeral head positioned in the glenoid cavity.
Function
Dynamic stabilisers during all shoulder movements. Particularly critical during the bench press and shoulder press when the shoulder is loaded in vulnerable positions.
Injury Mechanisms
High forces during resistance training → tendinitis, impingement, inflammation, and degeneration of contiguous tissue. Can result in tendon tearing under excessive or sudden loading.
Prevention
Balanced shoulder programming including internal and external rotation work. Progressive loading. Adequate warm-up. Avoid extreme ranges under maximal load.
kenhub.com
Topic 07 — Knees
Knee Joint & the Patella
Two Long Levers
Knee sits between upper and lower leg — two long levers that magnify forces on joint structures. Primary motion: flexion/extension in sagittal plane.
Patella Function
Holds quadriceps tendon away from knee axis of rotation → maintains moment arm length → preserves mechanical advantage of the quadriceps throughout ROM.
Patellar Tendinitis
Tenderness and swelling from excessive volume/intensity without progression. No inherent injury risk from exercises themselves — tendinitis is purely a programming problem.
Frontal/Transverse Loads
Rotation in frontal and transverse planes is largely prevented by ligamentous structures. Valgus collapse during landing — primary ACL injury mechanism — loads these planes dangerously.

Knee wraps add ~25 lbs (110 N) to squat through spring effect — they directly assist extension, not just stabilise. If used, limit to heaviest sets only.

kenhub.com
Topic 07 — Elbows & Wrists
Elbow — Injury Risk & Context
Overhead Exercise Risk
Primary injury risk with overhead exercises. However, risk is small compared to overhead sport (throwing, tennis, volleyball serve).
Other Injury Types
Elbow dislocation (gymnastics) · Traction apophysitis (diving, wrestling, hockey) · Epiphyseal growth plate concern in distal radius in young athletes.
Powerlifters — Low Risk
Study of 245 competitive powerlifters found extremely low incidence of elbow and wrist injury. One isolated triceps tendon tear case in middle-aged weightlifter.
Growth Plate Guidance
Survey of 500 sports medicine experts: most indicated that avoiding resistance training before physeal closure was not necessary.
kenhub.com
Topic 07 — Elbows & Wrists
Wrist — Injury Risk & Resistance Training Safety
Wrist Injury Rate
Wrist injury with weightlifting is very sporadic and only referred to through case studies. Bilateral distal biceps tendon rupture reported in one recreational weight trainer case.
Adolescent Concern
Limited data suggests possible distal radial epiphysis fracture in adolescent weightlifters. However, evidence base is very small — insufficient to recommend avoiding resistance training.
Overall RT Injury Rate
Resistance training has ~4 injuries per 1,000 hours of participation — among the lowest of all sport and physical conditioning activities.
Injury Rate Hierarchy
Lowest: cycling, walking, resistance training
Intermediate: running, aerobics
Highest: team sports

Resistance training is one of the safest forms of physical conditioning. Risk management — appropriate progression, technique, and recovery — not avoidance, is the correct approach.

Chapter 2 — All Key Equations

Complete Equation Reference

Mechanical Advantage
MA = MM / MR
>1.0 = advantage · <1.0 = disadvantage (most muscles)
Newton's 2nd Law
F = m × a
Force (N) = Mass (kg) × Acceleration (m·s⁻²)
Impulse–Momentum
F × Δt = m × Δv
Vector quantity — direction matters. Fixed impulse for a given jump height.
Work & Power
W = F × d  |  P = W/t
Scalars. J = N·m. W = J/s. No direction captured.
Torque
τ = F × d × sin θ
N·m. Peaks at 90°. Varies through entire ROM.
Gravity
Fg = m × 9.81
Weight in N. Moment arm = horizontal distance from joint.
Friction
FR = k × FN
k = friction coefficient. Static k > Dynamic k always.
Fluid Resistance
FR = k × v
Resistance ∝ velocity. Allows rapid acceleration — not isokinetic.
Elasticity
FR = k × x
Resistance ∝ stretch distance. Inverse of human torque curve.
Chapter 2 — Master Summary

Chapter 2 Key Takeaways

Topics 1–2: Musculature & Levers
  • Agonist, antagonist, synergist roles
  • 3 lever classes — MA = MM/MR
  • Most muscles: Class 3, MA <1.0
  • Farther insertion → more torque, less speed
Topics 3–4: Planes & Mechanics
  • Sagittal / Frontal / Transverse planes
  • F=ma → impulse-momentum derivation
  • W = Fd · P = W/t · τ = Fd sin θ
  • Weightlifting > powerlifting in power output
Topic 5: Strength Factors
  • Neural: recruitment + rate coding
  • Force ∝ CSA not volume
  • Resting length = max force
  • Eccentric > isometric > concentric
Topic 6: Resistance Sources
  • Gravity: horizontal distance = moment arm
  • Inertia: more early, less late in ROM
  • Fluid: no eccentric; not isokinetic
  • Elastic: inverse of human torque curve
Topic 7: Joint Safety
  • 85–90% herniations: L4/L5 or L5/S1
  • Neutral spine minimises disk stress
  • IAP: diaphragm + deep abdominals
  • RT = lowest injury rate of all activities
Exam Traps
  • Powerlifting ≠ highest power output
  • Elastic bands: peak resistance when airborne
  • Fluid machines: not truly isokinetic
  • Titin = 3rd contractile element
  • Valsalva ≠ only way to generate IAP
